summary: Gruber traces the evolution of Textile from Dean Allen's original web tool into a broader ecosystem of implementations, highlighting the important distinction between Textile as a markup language and Textile as software. He notes how Brad Choate's MT-Textile plugin for Movable Type expanded the syntax beyond the original, creating ambiguity about what constitutes 'official' Textile. The good news is that Allen and Choate are collaborating to unify the language. The post's second half pivots to a plea for help finding a job for Brad Choate's friend Ron Pacheco, which becomes an occasion for Gruber to deliver a pointed critique of the American health insurance system's cruelty toward the uninsured.
key_points: Textile is both a markup language and a software implementation — an important distinction that gets blurred as multiple implementations diverge
The organic, seat-of-the-pants growth of Textile (PHP original, Perl plugin, Python port) shows how open-source tools evolve through individual initiative
Dean Allen and Brad Choate collaborating on syntax improvements resolves the fragmentation problem
The U.S. health insurance system's employer-dependent model creates catastrophic gaps when people lose jobs, especially for those with pre-existing conditions
money_quotes: That's an important but easily overlooked distinction.

The whole thing is a little seat-of-the-pants (and I mean that in a good way): Dean just wanted to write a tool to make it easier to write for the web; Brad just wanted to use Dean's clever idea with Movable Type; and Mark ports software to Python for kicks.
Those of you who live in civilized nations may not be aware of the depraved state of health insurance here in the U.S.
Worse is what happens if you ever experience a discontinuity in coverage.
Anything that happens when you're without insurance — illness or accident — will be deemed a 'pre-existing condition' and may not be covered even if you obtain health insurance again.
I have some personal experience with exactly this sort of situation, and though I do not know Mr. Pacheco, I know how he must feel.
This is not a sob story (it makes me angry more than anything else, frankly).
key_insight: The organic growth of Textile illustrates how open-source tools evolve through individual passion projects, but the post's real punch comes from Gruber's furious aside on how America's employer-tied health insurance system turns job loss into a medical catastrophe.
